{{Short description|Kofun period burial mound in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name =Monju-in Nishi Kofun | native_name =文殊院西古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Monjuin Nishi Kofun, entrance-4.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Monju-in Nishi Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Monju-in Nishi Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|30|14|N|135|50|33|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Sakurai, Nara]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|30|14|N|135|50|33|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Monju-in Nishi Kofun'''|文殊院西古墳|}} is an late [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of [[Sakurai, Nara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1923 and elevated to a Special National Historic Site in 1952.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1944|title=文殊院西古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Monju-in Nishi Kofun is located in the western end of the Abe Hills in the southern part of the [[Nara Basin]], within the grounds of the [[Abe Monju-in]] temple. It is known that it was already open during the [[Muromachi period]], and the tumulus has been leveled, leaving only the exposed [[burial chamber]]. No [[archaeological excavation]] has been carried out to date. The original shape of the tumulus is unclear, but it is estimated to be an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of about 25 to 30 meters and a height of about six meters. No ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones or ''[[haniwa]]'' clay figures have been found. The burial chamber is orientated to the south, and is made of five tiers of dressed [[granite]] stones, with a huge [[monolith]] forming the ceiling. The center of the ceiling stone is shallowly dug into a dome shape to prevent water droplets that accumulate on the ceiling stone from falling onto the coffin. The 7.39 meter long passageway is lined with a single tier of huge stones. Traces of plaster remains on the walls. Grooves carved into the ceiling stone and side walls of the passageway opening, suggest the existence of a door between the passageway and burial chamber itself. No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The construction date of the burial chamber is estimated to be around the second half of the 7th century, towards the end of the Kofun period. It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Kofun File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|3D image of burial chamber File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-3.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the back wall)） File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-4.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the passageway) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-4.jpg|Passageway (towards the entrance) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-3.jpg|Passageway (towards the burial chamber) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, entrance-3.jpg|Entry </gallery>
In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber burial mound may have existed apart from this burial mound.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category|Monjuin Nishi Kofun}} *[https://sakurai-kankou.jimdoweb.com/名所旧跡/磐余の道-安倍文殊院ほか/文殊院西古墳/ Sakurai City Tourist Association home page]{{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.pref.nara.jp/miryoku/ikasu-nara/bunkashigen/main00104.htmlNara Prefecture History and Cultural database]{{in lang|ja}}

In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber burial mound may have existed apart from this burial mound.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Monju-in Nishi Kofun'''|文殊院西古墳|}} is an late [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of [[Sakurai, Nara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1923 and elevated to a Special National Historic Site in 1952.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1944|title=文殊院西古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Monju-in Nishi Kofun is located in the western end of the Abe Hills in the southern part of the [[Nara Basin]], within the grounds of the [[Abe Monju-in]] temple. It is known that it was already open during the [[Muromachi period]], and the tumulus has been leveled, leaving only the exposed [[burial chamber]]. No [[archaeological excavation]] has been carried out to date. The original shape of the tumulus is unclear, but it is estimated to be an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of about 25 to 30 meters and a height of about six meters. No ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones or ''[[haniwa]]'' clay figures have been found. The burial chamber is orientated to the south, and is made of five tiers of dressed [[granite]] stones, with a huge [[monolith]] forming the ceiling. The center of the ceiling stone is shallowly dug into a dome shape to prevent water droplets that accumulate on the ceiling stone from falling onto the coffin. The 7.39 meter long passageway is lined with a single tier of huge stones. Traces of plaster remains on the walls. Grooves carved into the ceiling stone and side walls of the passageway opening, suggest the existence of a door between the passageway and burial chamber itself. No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The construction date of the burial chamber is estimated to be around the second half of the 7th century, towards the end of the Kofun period. It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber burial mound may have existed apart from this burial mound.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mound in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name =Monju-in Nishi Kofun | native_name =文殊院西古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Monjuin Nishi Kofun, entrance-4.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Monju-in Nishi Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Monju-in Nishi Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|30|14|N|135|50|33|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Sakurai, Nara]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|30|14|N|135|50|33|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Monju-in Nishi Kofun'''|文殊院西古墳|}} is an late [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of [[Sakurai, Nara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1923 and elevated to a Special National Historic Site in 1952.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1944|title=文殊院西古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Monju-in Nishi Kofun is located in the western end of the Abe Hills in the southern part of the [[Nara Basin]], within the grounds of the [[Abe Monju-in]] temple. It is known that it was already open during the [[Muromachi period]], and the tumulus has been leveled, leaving only the exposed [[burial chamber]]. No [[archaeological excavation]] has been carried out to date. The original shape of the tumulus is unclear, but it is estimated to be an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of about 25 to 30 meters and a height of about six meters. No ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones or ''[[haniwa]]'' clay figures have been found. The burial chamber is orientated to the south, and is made of five tiers of dressed [[granite]] stones, with a huge [[monolith]] forming the ceiling. The center of the ceiling stone is shallowly dug into a dome shape to prevent water droplets that accumulate on the ceiling stone from falling onto the coffin. The 7.39 meter long passageway is lined with a single tier of huge stones. Traces of plaster remains on the walls. Grooves carved into the ceiling stone and side walls of the passageway opening, suggest the existence of a door between the passageway and burial chamber itself. No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The construction date of the burial chamber is estimated to be around the second half of the 7th century, towards the end of the Kofun period. It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Kofun File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|3D image of burial chamber File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-3.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the back wall)） File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-4.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the passageway) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-4.jpg|Passageway (towards the entrance) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-3.jpg|Passageway (towards the burial chamber) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, entrance-3.jpg|Entry </gallery>
In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber burial mound may have existed apart from this burial mound.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Monju-in Nishi Kofun'''|文殊院西古墳|}} is an late [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of [[Sakurai, Nara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1923 and elevated to a Special National Historic Site in 1952.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1944|title=文殊院西古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Monju-in Nishi Kofun is located in the western end of the Abe Hills in the southern part of the [[Nara Basin]], within the grounds of the [[Abe Monju-in]] temple. It is known that it was already open during the [[Muromachi period]], and the tumulus has been leveled, leaving only the exposed [[burial chamber]]. No [[archaeological excavation]] has been carried out to date. The original shape of the tumulus is unclear, but it is estimated to be an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of about 25 to 30 meters and a height of about six meters. No ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones or ''[[haniwa]]'' clay figures have been found. The burial chamber is orientated to the south, and is made of five tiers of dressed [[granite]] stones, with a huge [[monolith]] forming the ceiling. The center of the ceiling stone is shallowly dug into a dome shape to prevent water droplets that accumulate on the ceiling stone from falling onto the coffin. The 7.39 meter long passageway is lined with a single tier of huge stones. Traces of plaster remains on the walls. Grooves carved into the ceiling stone and side walls of the passageway opening, suggest the existence of a door between the passageway and burial chamber itself. No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The construction date of the burial chamber is estimated to be around the second half of the 7th century, towards the end of the Kofun period. It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Kofun File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|3D image of burial chamber File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-3.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the back wall)） File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-4.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the passageway) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-4.jpg|Passageway (towards the entrance) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-3.jpg|Passageway (towards the burial chamber) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, entrance-3.jpg|Entry </gallery>
In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber burial mound may have existed apart from this burial mound.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

No [[archaeological excavation]] has been carried out to date. The original shape of the tumulus is unclear, but it is estimated to be an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of about 25 to 30 meters and a height of about six meters. No ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones or ''[[haniwa]]'' clay figures have been found. The burial chamber is orientated to the south, and is made of five tiers of dressed [[granite]] stones, with a huge [[monolith]] forming the ceiling. The center of the ceiling stone is shallowly dug into a dome shape to prevent water droplets that accumulate on the ceiling stone from falling onto the coffin. The 7.39 meter long passageway is lined with a single tier of huge stones. Traces of plaster remains on the walls. Grooves carved into the ceiling stone and side walls of the passageway opening, suggest the existence of a door between the passageway and burial chamber itself. No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The construction date of the burial chamber is estimated to be around the second half of the 7th century, towards the end of the Kofun period. It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Kofun File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|3D image of burial chamber File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-3.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the back wall)） File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-4.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the passageway) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-4.jpg|Passageway (towards the entrance) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-3.jpg|Passageway (towards the burial chamber) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, entrance-3.jpg|Entry </gallery>
In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber burial mound may have existed apart from this burial mound.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The construction date of the burial chamber is estimated to be around the second half of the 7th century, towards the end of the Kofun period. It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber burial mound may have existed apart from this burial mound.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Kofun File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|3D image of burial chamber File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-3.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the back wall)） File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-4.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the passageway) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-4.jpg|Passageway (towards the entrance) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-3.jpg|Passageway (towards the burial chamber) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, entrance-3.jpg|Entry </gallery>
In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber burial mound may have existed apart from this burial mound.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Monju-in Nishi Kofun'''|文殊院西古墳|}} is an late [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of [[Sakurai, Nara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1923 and elevated to a Special National Historic Site in 1952.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1944|title=文殊院西古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Monju-in Nishi Kofun is located in the western end of the Abe Hills in the southern part of the [[Nara Basin]], within the grounds of the [[Abe Monju-in]] temple. It is known that it was already open during the [[Muromachi period]], and the tumulus has been leveled, leaving only the exposed [[burial chamber]]. No [[archaeological excavation]] has been carried out to date. The original shape of the tumulus is unclear, but it is estimated to be an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of about 25 to 30 meters and a height of about six meters. No ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones or ''[[haniwa]]'' clay figures have been found. The burial chamber is orientated to the south, and is made of five tiers of dressed [[granite]] stones, with a huge [[monolith]] forming the ceiling. The center of the ceiling stone is shallowly dug into a dome shape to prevent water droplets that accumulate on the ceiling stone from falling onto the coffin. The 7.39 meter long passageway is lined with a single tier of huge stones. Traces of plaster remains on the walls. Grooves carved into the ceiling stone and side walls of the passageway opening, suggest the existence of a door between the passageway and burial chamber itself. No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The construction date of the burial chamber is estimated to be around the second half of the 7th century, towards the end of the Kofun period. It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Kofun File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|3D image of burial chamber File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-3.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the back wall)） File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-4.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the passageway) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-4.jpg|Passageway (towards the entrance) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-3.jpg|Passageway (towards the burial chamber) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, entrance-3.jpg|Entry </gallery>
In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber burial mound may have existed apart from this burial mound.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Monju-in Nishi Kofun'''|文殊院西古墳|}} is an late [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of [[Sakurai, Nara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1923 and elevated to a Special National Historic Site in 1952.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1944|title=文殊院西古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Monju-in Nishi Kofun is located in the western end of the Abe Hills in the southern part of the [[Nara Basin]], within the grounds of the [[Abe Monju-in]] temple. It is known that it was already open during the [[Muromachi period]], and the tumulus has been leveled, leaving only the exposed [[burial chamber]]. No [[archaeological excavation]] has been carried out to date. The original shape of the tumulus is unclear, but it is estimated to be an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of about 25 to 30 meters and a height of about six meters. No ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones or ''[[haniwa]]'' clay figures have been found. The burial chamber is orientated to the south, and is made of five tiers of dressed [[granite]] stones, with a huge [[monolith]] forming the ceiling. The center of the ceiling stone is shallowly dug into a dome shape to prevent water droplets that accumulate on the ceiling stone from falling onto the coffin. The 7.39 meter long passageway is lined with a single tier of huge stones. Traces of plaster remains on the walls. Grooves carved into the ceiling stone and side walls of the passageway opening, suggest the existence of a door between the passageway and burial chamber itself. No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The construction date of the burial chamber is estimated to be around the second half of the 7th century, towards the end of the Kofun period. It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber burial mound may have existed apart from this burial mound.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1923 and elevated to a Special National Historic Site in 1952.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1944|title=文殊院西古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Monju-in Nishi Kofun is located in the western end of the Abe Hills in the southern part of the [[Nara Basin]], within the grounds of the [[Abe Monju-in]] temple. It is known that it was already open during the [[Muromachi period]], and the tumulus has been leveled, leaving only the exposed [[burial chamber]]. No [[archaeological excavation]] has been carried out to date. The original shape of the tumulus is unclear, but it is estimated to be an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of about 25 to 30 meters and a height of about six meters. No ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones or ''[[haniwa]]'' clay figures have been found. The burial chamber is orientated to the south, and is made of five tiers of dressed [[granite]] stones, with a huge [[monolith]] forming the ceiling. The center of the ceiling stone is shallowly dug into a dome shape to prevent water droplets that accumulate on the ceiling stone from falling onto the coffin. The 7.39 meter long passageway is lined with a single tier of huge stones. Traces of plaster remains on the walls. Grooves carved into the ceiling stone and side walls of the passageway opening, suggest the existence of a door between the passageway and burial chamber itself. No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Grooves carved into the ceiling stone and side walls of the passageway opening, suggest the existence of a door between the passageway and burial chamber itself. No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The construction date of the burial chamber is estimated to be around the second half of the 7th century, towards the end of the Kofun period. It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber burial mound may have existed apart from this burial mound.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Monju-in Nishi Kofun'''|文殊院西古墳|}} is an late [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of [[Sakurai, Nara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1923 and elevated to a Special National Historic Site in 1952.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1944|title=文殊院西古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Monju-in Nishi Kofun is located in the western end of the Abe Hills in the southern part of the [[Nara Basin]], within the grounds of the [[Abe Monju-in]] temple. It is known that it was already open during the [[Muromachi period]], and the tumulus has been leveled, leaving only the exposed [[burial chamber]]. No [[archaeological excavation]] has been carried out to date. The original shape of the tumulus is unclear, but it is estimated to be an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of about 25 to 30 meters and a height of about six meters. No ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones or ''[[haniwa]]'' clay figures have been found. The burial chamber is orientated to the south, and is made of five tiers of dressed [[granite]] stones, with a huge [[monolith]] forming the ceiling. The center of the ceiling stone is shallowly dug into a dome shape to prevent water droplets that accumulate on the ceiling stone from falling onto the coffin. The 7.39 meter long passageway is lined with a single tier of huge stones. Traces of plaster remains on the walls. Grooves carved into the ceiling stone and side walls of the passageway opening, suggest the existence of a door between the passageway and burial chamber itself. No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The construction date of the burial chamber is estimated to be around the second half of the 7th century, towards the end of the Kofun period. It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mound in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name =Monju-in Nishi Kofun | native_name =文殊院西古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Monjuin Nishi Kofun, entrance-4.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Monju-in Nishi Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Monju-in Nishi Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|30|14|N|135|50|33|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Sakurai, Nara]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|30|14|N|135|50|33|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Monju-in Nishi Kofun'''|文殊院西古墳|}} is an late [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of [[Sakurai, Nara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1923 and elevated to a Special National Historic Site in 1952.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1944|title=文殊院西古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Monju-in Nishi Kofun is located in the western end of the Abe Hills in the southern part of the [[Nara Basin]], within the grounds of the [[Abe Monju-in]] temple. It is known that it was already open during the [[Muromachi period]], and the tumulus has been leveled, leaving only the exposed [[burial chamber]]. No [[archaeological excavation]] has been carried out to date. The original shape of the tumulus is unclear, but it is estimated to be an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of about 25 to 30 meters and a height of about six meters. No ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones or ''[[haniwa]]'' clay figures have been found. The burial chamber is orientated to the south, and is made of five tiers of dressed [[granite]] stones, with a huge [[monolith]] forming the ceiling. The center of the ceiling stone is shallowly dug into a dome shape to prevent water droplets that accumulate on the ceiling stone from falling onto the coffin. The 7.39 meter long passageway is lined with a single tier of huge stones. Traces of plaster remains on the walls. Grooves carved into the ceiling stone and side walls of the passageway opening, suggest the existence of a door between the passageway and burial chamber itself. No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The construction date of the burial chamber is estimated to be around the second half of the 7th century, towards the end of the Kofun period. It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Kofun File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|3D image of burial chamber File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-3.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the back wall)） File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-4.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the passageway) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-4.jpg|Passageway (towards the entrance) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-3.jpg|Passageway (towards the burial chamber) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, entrance-3.jpg|Entry </gallery>
In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber burial mound may have existed apart from this burial mound.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]]

In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber burial mound may have existed apart from this burial mound.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mound in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name =Monju-in Nishi Kofun | native_name =文殊院西古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Monjuin Nishi Kofun, entrance-4.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Monju-in Nishi Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Monju-in Nishi Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|30|14|N|135|50|33|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Sakurai, Nara]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|30|14|N|135|50|33|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Monju-in Nishi Kofun'''|文殊院西古墳|}} is an late [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of [[Sakurai, Nara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1923 and elevated to a Special National Historic Site in 1952.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1944|title=文殊院西古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Monju-in Nishi Kofun is located in the western end of the Abe Hills in the southern part of the [[Nara Basin]], within the grounds of the [[Abe Monju-in]] temple. It is known that it was already open during the [[Muromachi period]], and the tumulus has been leveled, leaving only the exposed [[burial chamber]]. No [[archaeological excavation]] has been carried out to date. The original shape of the tumulus is unclear, but it is estimated to be an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of about 25 to 30 meters and a height of about six meters. No ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones or ''[[haniwa]]'' clay figures have been found. The burial chamber is orientated to the south, and is made of five tiers of dressed [[granite]] stones, with a huge [[monolith]] forming the ceiling. The center of the ceiling stone is shallowly dug into a dome shape to prevent water droplets that accumulate on the ceiling stone from falling onto the coffin. The 7.39 meter long passageway is lined with a single tier of huge stones. Traces of plaster remains on the walls. Grooves carved into the ceiling stone and side walls of the passageway opening, suggest the existence of a door between the passageway and burial chamber itself. No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The construction date of the burial chamber is estimated to be around the second half of the 7th century, towards the end of the Kofun period. It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Kofun File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|3D image of burial chamber File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-3.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the back wall)） File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-4.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the passageway) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-4.jpg|Passageway (towards the entrance) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-3.jpg|Passageway (towards the burial chamber) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, entrance-3.jpg|Entry </gallery>
In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber burial mound may have existed apart from this burial mound.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period burial mound in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name =Monju-in Nishi Kofun | native_name =文殊院西古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Monjuin Nishi Kofun, entrance-4.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Monju-in Nishi Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Monju-in Nishi Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|30|14|N|135|50|33|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Sakurai, Nara]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|30|14|N|135|50|33|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Monju-in Nishi Kofun'''|文殊院西古墳|}} is an late [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of [[Sakurai, Nara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1923 and elevated to a Special National Historic Site in 1952.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1944|title=文殊院西古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Monju-in Nishi Kofun is located in the western end of the Abe Hills in the southern part of the [[Nara Basin]], within the grounds of the [[Abe Monju-in]] temple. It is known that it was already open during the [[Muromachi period]], and the tumulus has been leveled, leaving only the exposed [[burial chamber]]. No [[archaeological excavation]] has been carried out to date. The original shape of the tumulus is unclear, but it is estimated to be an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of about 25 to 30 meters and a height of about six meters. No ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones or ''[[haniwa]]'' clay figures have been found. The burial chamber is orientated to the south, and is made of five tiers of dressed [[granite]] stones, with a huge [[monolith]] forming the ceiling. The center of the ceiling stone is shallowly dug into a dome shape to prevent water droplets that accumulate on the ceiling stone from falling onto the coffin. The 7.39 meter long passageway is lined with a single tier of huge stones. Traces of plaster remains on the walls. Grooves carved into the ceiling stone and side walls of the passageway opening, suggest the existence of a door between the passageway and burial chamber itself. No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The construction date of the burial chamber is estimated to be around the second half of the 7th century, towards the end of the Kofun period. It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Kofun File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|3D image of burial chamber File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-3.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the back wall)） File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-4.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the passageway) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-4.jpg|Passageway (towards the entrance) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-3.jpg|Passageway (towards the burial chamber) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, entrance-3.jpg|Entry </gallery>
In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber burial mound may have existed apart from this burial mound.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber burial mound may have existed apart from this burial mound.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Monju-in Nishi Kofun'''|文殊院西古墳|}} is an late [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of [[Sakurai, Nara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1923 and elevated to a Special National Historic Site in 1952.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1944|title=文殊院西古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Monju-in Nishi Kofun is located in the western end of the Abe Hills in the southern part of the [[Nara Basin]], within the grounds of the [[Abe Monju-in]] temple. It is known that it was already open during the [[Muromachi period]], and the tumulus has been leveled, leaving only the exposed [[burial chamber]]. No [[archaeological excavation]] has been carried out to date. The original shape of the tumulus is unclear, but it is estimated to be an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of about 25 to 30 meters and a height of about six meters. No ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones or ''[[haniwa]]'' clay figures have been found. The burial chamber is orientated to the south, and is made of five tiers of dressed [[granite]] stones, with a huge [[monolith]] forming the ceiling. The center of the ceiling stone is shallowly dug into a dome shape to prevent water droplets that accumulate on the ceiling stone from falling onto the coffin. The 7.39 meter long passageway is lined with a single tier of huge stones. Traces of plaster remains on the walls. Grooves carved into the ceiling stone and side walls of the passageway opening, suggest the existence of a door between the passageway and burial chamber itself. No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The construction date of the burial chamber is estimated to be around the second half of the 7th century, towards the end of the Kofun period. It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber burial mound may have existed apart from this burial mound.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Monju-in Nishi Kofun'''|文殊院西古墳|}} is an late [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|burial mound]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of [[Sakurai, Nara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1923 and elevated to a Special National Historic Site in 1952.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1944|title=文殊院西古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Monju-in Nishi Kofun is located in the western end of the Abe Hills in the southern part of the [[Nara Basin]], within the grounds of the [[Abe Monju-in]] temple. It is known that it was already open during the [[Muromachi period]], and the tumulus has been leveled, leaving only the exposed [[burial chamber]]. No [[archaeological excavation]] has been carried out to date. The original shape of the tumulus is unclear, but it is estimated to be an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of about 25 to 30 meters and a height of about six meters. No ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones or ''[[haniwa]]'' clay figures have been found. The burial chamber is orientated to the south, and is made of five tiers of dressed [[granite]] stones, with a huge [[monolith]] forming the ceiling. The center of the ceiling stone is shallowly dug into a dome shape to prevent water droplets that accumulate on the ceiling stone from falling onto the coffin. The 7.39 meter long passageway is lined with a single tier of huge stones. Traces of plaster remains on the walls. Grooves carved into the ceiling stone and side walls of the passageway opening, suggest the existence of a door between the passageway and burial chamber itself. No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The construction date of the burial chamber is estimated to be around the second half of the 7th century, towards the end of the Kofun period. It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Kofun File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|3D image of burial chamber File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-3.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the back wall)） File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-4.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the passageway) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-4.jpg|Passageway (towards the entrance) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-3.jpg|Passageway (towards the burial chamber) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, entrance-3.jpg|Entry </gallery>
In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber burial mound may have existed apart from this burial mound.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Monju-in Nishi Kofun'''|文殊院西古墳|}} is an late [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of [[Sakurai, Nara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1923 and elevated to a Special National Historic Site in 1952.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1944|title=文殊院西古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Monju-in Nishi Kofun is located in the western end of the Abe Hills in the southern part of the [[Nara Basin]], within the grounds of the [[Abe Monju-in]] temple. It is known that it was already open during the [[Muromachi period]], and the tumulus has been leveled, leaving only the exposed [[burial chamber]]. No [[archaeological excavation]] has been carried out to date. The original shape of the tumulus is unclear, but it is estimated to be an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of about 25 to 30 meters and a height of about six meters. No ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones or ''[[haniwa]]'' clay figures have been found. The burial chamber is orientated to the south, and is made of five tiers of dressed [[granite]] stones, with a huge [[monolith]] forming the ceiling. The center of the ceiling stone is shallowly dug into a dome shape to prevent water droplets that accumulate on the ceiling stone from falling onto the coffin. The 7.39 meter long passageway is lined with a single tier of huge stones. Traces of plaster remains on the walls. Grooves carved into the ceiling stone and side walls of the passageway opening, suggest the existence of a door between the passageway and burial chamber itself. No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The construction date of the burial chamber is estimated to be around the second half of the 7th century, towards the end of the Kofun period. It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber Kofun may have existed apart from this Kofun.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Monju-in Nishi Kofun'''|文殊院西古墳|}} is an late [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of [[Sakurai, Nara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1923 and elevated to a Special National Historic Site in 1952.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1944|title=文殊院西古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Monju-in Nishi Kofun is located in the western end of the Abe Hills in the southern part of the [[Nara Basin]], within the grounds of the [[Abe Monju-in]] temple. It is known that it was already open during the [[Muromachi period]], and the tumulus has been leveled, leaving only the exposed [[burial chamber]]. No [[archaeological excavation]] has been carried out to date. The original shape of the tumulus is unclear, but it is estimated to be an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of about 25 to 30 meters and a height of about six meters. No ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones or ''[[haniwa]]'' clay figures have been found. The burial chamber is orientated to the south, and is made of five tiers of dressed [[granite]] stones, with a huge [[monolith]] forming the ceiling. The center of the ceiling stone is shallowly dug into a dome shape to prevent water droplets that accumulate on the ceiling stone from falling onto the coffin. The 7.39 meter long passageway is lined with a single tier of huge stones. Traces of plaster remains on the walls. Grooves carved into the ceiling stone and side walls of the passageway opening, suggest the existence of a door between the passageway and burial chamber itself. No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The construction date of the burial chamber is estimated to be around the second half of the 7th century, towards the end of the Kofun period. It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Kofun File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|3D image of burial chamber File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-3.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the back wall)） File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-4.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the passageway) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-4.jpg|Passageway (towards the entrance) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-3.jpg|Passageway (towards the burial chamber) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, entrance-3.jpg|Entry </gallery>
In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber Kofun may have existed apart from this Kofun.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} | name =Monju-in Nishi Kofun | native_name =文殊院西古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Monjuin Nishi Kofun, entrance-4.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Monju-in Nishi Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Monju-in Nishi Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|30|14|N|135|50|33|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Sakurai, Nara]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|30|14|N|135|50|33|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Monju-in Nishi Kofun'''|文殊院西古墳|}} is an late [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of [[Sakurai, Nara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1923 and elevated to a Special National Historic Site in 1952.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1944|title=文殊院西古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Monju-in Nishi Kofun is located in the western end of the Abe Hills in the southern part of the [[Nara Basin]], within the grounds of the [[Abe Monju-in]] temple. It is known that it was already open during the [[Muromachi period]], and the tumulus has been leveled, leaving only the exposed [[burial chamber]]. No [[archaeological excavation]] has been carried out to date. The original shape of the tumulus is unclear, but it is estimated to be an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of about 25 to 30 meters and a height of about six meters. No ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones or ''[[haniwa]]'' clay figures have been found. The burial chamber is orientated to the south, and is made of five tiers of dressed [[granite]] stones, with a huge [[monolith]] forming the ceiling. The center of the ceiling stone is shallowly dug into a dome shape to prevent water droplets that accumulate on the ceiling stone from falling onto the coffin. The 7.39 meter long passageway is lined with a single tier of huge stones. Traces of plaster remains on the walls. Grooves carved into the ceiling stone and side walls of the passageway opening, suggest the existence of a door between the passageway and burial chamber itself. No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The construction date of the burial chamber is estimated to be around the second half of the 7th century, towards the end of the Kofun period. It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Kofun File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|3D image of burial chamber File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-3.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the back wall)） File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-4.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the passageway) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-4.jpg|Passageway (towards the entrance) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-3.jpg|Passageway (towards the burial chamber) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, entrance-3.jpg|Entry </gallery>
In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber Kofun may have existed apart from this Kofun.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]]

{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} | name =Monju-in Nishi Kofun | native_name =文殊院西古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Monjuin Nishi Kofun, entrance-4.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Monju-in Nishi Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Monju-in Nishi Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|30|14|N|135|50|33|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Sakurai, Nara]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|30|14|N|135|50|33|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Monju-in Nishi Kofun'''|文殊院西古墳|}} is an late [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Takada neighborhood of the city of [[Sakurai, Nara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1923 and elevated to a Special National Historic Site in 1952.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1944|title=文殊院西古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== The Monju-in Nishi Kofun is located in the western end of the Abe Hills in the southern part of the [[Nara Basin]], within the grounds of the [[Abe Monju-in]] temple. It is known that it was already open during the [[Muromachi period]], and the tumulus has been leveled, leaving only the exposed [[burial chamber]]. No [[archaeological excavation]] has been carried out to date. The original shape of the tumulus is unclear, but it is estimated to be an {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|||}}-style circular tumulus with a diameter of about 25 to 30 meters and a height of about six meters. No ''[[fukiishi]]'' roofing stones or ''[[haniwa]]'' clay figures have been found. The burial chamber is orientated to the south, and is made of five tiers of dressed [[granite]] stones, with a huge [[monolith]] forming the ceiling. The center of the ceiling stone is shallowly dug into a dome shape to prevent water droplets that accumulate on the ceiling stone from falling onto the coffin. The 7.39 meter long passageway is lined with a single tier of huge stones. Traces of plaster remains on the walls. Grooves carved into the ceiling stone and side walls of the passageway opening, suggest the existence of a door between the passageway and burial chamber itself. No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The construction date of the burial chamber is estimated to be around the second half of the 7th century, towards the end of the Kofun period. It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Kofun File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|3D image of burial chamber File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-3.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the back wall)） File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, genshitsu-4.jpg|The burial chamber (towards the passageway) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-4.jpg|Passageway (towards the entrance) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, sendou-3.jpg|Passageway (towards the burial chamber) File:Monjuin Nishi Kofun, entrance-3.jpg|Entry </gallery>
In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber Kofun may have existed apart from this Kofun.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]]

Grooves carved into the ceiling stone and side walls of the passageway opening, suggest the existence of a door between the passageway and burial chamber itself. No [[grave goods]] have been found at the site.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The construction date of the burial chamber is estimated to be around the second half of the 7th century, towards the end of the Kofun period. It is considered noteworthy for the craftsmanship of the cutting of the stone blocks used in the burial chamber. This tomb is certain to be the tomb of a powerful chief of the [[Abe clan]], and the most likely theory is that the deceased was [[Abe no Kurahashimaro]] (Abe no Uchimaro, died in 649), who served as [[Sadaijin]] during the [[Taika Reforms]] in 645. Despite being the head of the government at the time, it is recorded that constructed a tomb on a scale that exceeded the regulations of the Taika Reforms, making his tomb a point of contention.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

In addition, there are stones of the same shape as the burial chamber scattered around the grounds of Abe Monjuiin, suggesting the possibility that a similar stone chamber Kofun may have existed apart from this Kofun.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The tumulus is about 1.5 kilometers south of [[Sakurai Station (Nara)|Sakurai Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Osaka Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
